elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce novel methods for adapting diffusion models under differential privacy (DP) constraints, enabling privacy-preserving style and content transfer without fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional approaches to private adaptation, such as DP-SGD, incur significant computational overhead and degrade model performance when applied to large, complex models.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach instead leverages embedding-based techniques: Universal Guidance and Textual Inversion (TI), adapted with differentially private mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-004: We apply these methods to Stable Diffusion for style adaptation using two private datasets: a collection of artworks by a single artist and pictograms from the Paris 2024 Olympics.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experimental results show that the TI-based adaptation achieves superior fidelity in style transfer, even under strong privacy guarantees, while both methods maintain high privacy resilience by employing calibrated noise and subsampling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings demonstrate a feasible and efficient pathway for privacy-preserving diffusion model adaptation, balancing data protection with the fidelity of generated images, and offer insights into embedding-driven methods for DP in generative AI applications.
elements.sent-2411-007: In recent years, diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising, song2020score , particularly latent diffusion models~\cite{rombach2022high , have spearheaded high quality text-to-image generation, and have been widely adopted by researchers and the general public alike.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Trained on massive datasets like LAION-5B~\cite{schuhmann2022laion , these models have developed a broad understanding of visual concepts, enabling new creative and practical applications.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Notably, tools like Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high, podell2023sdxl have been made readily accessible for general use.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Building on this foundation, efficient adaptation methods such as parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)~\cite{hu2022lora,von-platen-etal-2022-diffusers,ruiz2023dreambooth , guidance based approaches~\cite{ho2021classifierfree, kim2022diffusionclip, bansal2023universal , and pseudo-word generation~\cite{gal2023an enable users to leverage this extensive pretraining for customizing models that can specialize on downstream tasks with smaller datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the rapid adoption of diffusion models has also raised significant privacy, ethical and legal concerns.
elements.sent-2411-012: One critical issue is the vulnerability of these models to privacy attacks, from membership inference~\cite{duan2023diffusion , where an attacker determines whether a specific data point was used to train a particular model, to data extraction~\cite{carlini2023extracting , which enables an attacker to reconstruct particular images from the training dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This issue is even more severe during the fine-tuning phase where the model is fine-tuned on smaller specialized datasets from a possibly different domain and each data record has more impact on the final model.
elements.sent-2411-014: This risk underscores the importance of privacy-preserving technologies, particularly as diffusion models often rely on vast datasets scraped from the internet without explicit consent from content owners.
elements.sent-2411-015: Copyright concerns further complicate the landscape~\cite{vyas2023provable .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, diffusion models can closely reproduce original artworks without credit or compensation to the artist, as evident in data extraction attacks \cite{carlini2023extracting .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Such concerns intensify the debate on balancing technological advancement with the rights and privacy of content owners, underscoring the need for clearer ethical and legal guidelines in the training and deployment of generative models.
elements.sent-2411-018: Differential privacy~(DP)~\cite{dwork2006calibrating, dwork2006differential is a widely adopted approach to addressing these challenges, where controlled noise is added during training or inference to prevent information leakage from individual data points while still enabling the model to learn effectively from the overall dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The standard method for ensuring DP in deep learning is Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD)~\cite{abadi2016deep , which modifies traditional SGD by adding noise to clipped gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-020: While, in theory, DP-SGD can be applied to train diffusion models, it poses several significant practical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-021: First, it is well known that DP-SGD introduces considerable computational and memory overhead due to the need for per-sample gradient clipping, which is crucial for bounding sensitivity~\cite{dwork2006calibrating, abadi2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Second, DP-SGD is fundamentally incompatible with batch-wise operations, such as batch normalization, since these operations inherently link samples together, rendering sensitivity analysis infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, training a large network with DP-SGD typically leads to significant performance degradation (i.e. the curse of dimensionality), even under relatively lenient privacy budgets.
elements.sent-2411-024: To the best of our knowledge, existing diffusion models trained with DP-SGD are constrained to relatively small-scale datasets~\cite{dockhorn2023differentially, ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-025: As a result, recent research has increasingly turned to alternative strategies for adapting diffusion models in a privacy-preserving manner without the need for training from scratch under DP-SGD.
elements.sent-2411-026: A promising approach leverages large, publicly pre-trained models, adapting them to new domains under differential privacy (DP) constraints, which allows models to capitalize on existing representational strengths while avoiding the computational and memory burdens of full DP training~\cite{ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Similarly, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as DP-LoRA~\cite{yu2021differentially , fine-tune only a subset of parameters, allowing for efficient, targeted adaptation with limited privacy costs.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Methods like DP-RDM (Differentially Private Retrieval-Augmented Diffusion Model)~\cite{lebensold2024dp illustrate this trend by enabling model adaptation through a retrieval mechanism that conditions image generation on private data retrieved at inference time, avoiding direct model updates.}
elements.sent-2411-029: These approaches collectively demonstrate how large models can be adapted privately, circumventing the complexities and high costs associated with full DP training.
elements.sent-2411-030: Meanwhile, alternative methods such as Universal Guidance~\cite{bansal2023universal and Textual Inversion (TI)~\cite{gal2023an enable model adaptation to specific content or style without modifying the diffusion model itself, instead relying on external embedding vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Universal Guidance’s style guidance (SG) mechanism generates a target CLIP embedding by passing the reference image through a CLIP image encoder.
elements.sent-2411-032: During the reverse diffusion process, the cosine distance between this target embedding and the CLIP embedding of the generated image is minimized, guiding the generation process to match the desired style.
elements.sent-2411-033: TI, in contrast, learns a new token embedding representing a set of target images, which is then incorporated into the text prompt to adapt the model to the style or content associated with those images.
elements.sent-2411-034: Both SG and TI avoid direct model optimization, reducing memory and computation demands while also involving fewer trainable parameters, making them highly compatible with DP constraints on smaller datasets.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this work, we propose two novel privacy-preserving adaptation methods for smaller datasets, leveraging SG and TI to bypass the need for DP-SGD or DP-PEFT approaches that require extensive model updating.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our first approach adapts Universal Guidance for privacy by using a differentially private CLIP embedding.
elements.sent-2411-037: Although SG is typically applied to a single target image, we extend it to handle multiple images under DP by computing an embedding for each target image and aggregating these embeddings via a centroid calculation.
elements.sent-2411-038: To ensure differential privacy, we add calibrated noise to the centroid, which protects individual data points in the dataset while preserving the model’s guidance capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our second approach enhances Textual Inversion (TI) with differential privacy.
elements.sent-2411-040: While standard TI compresses training data into a single, low-dimensional vector, offering some intrinsic privacy benefits, it lacks the rigorous guarantees required by DP.
elements.sent-2411-041: We address this by introducing a private variant of TI, in which we decouple interactions among samples by learning a separate embedding for each target image.
elements.sent-2411-042: These embeddings are then aggregated into a noisy centroid, similar to our SG approach, ensuring differential privacy while preserving TI’s data compression advantages.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our experimental results underscore the practicality and effectiveness of the proposed differentially private embedding-based adaptation methods, with Textual Inversion (TI) proving particularly robust for preserving stylistic fidelity even under privacy constraints.
elements.sent-2411-044: In applying our approach to two distinct datasets--a private collection of artworks by {\eve and pictograms from the Paris 2024 Olympics~\cite{Paris2024Pictograms --we demonstrate that differentially private TI can successfully capture nuanced stylistic elements while maintaining privacy guarantees.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Our findings reveal a trade-off between privacy levels (determined by the DP parameter $\varepsilon$) and image quality, where lower $\varepsilon$ values yield diminished stylistic fidelity, yet still preserve the target style under moderate noise.
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, subsampling offers an effective means of improving privacy resilience by controlling sensitivity to individual data points, significantly mitigating the impact of added noise on image quality.
elements.sent-2411-047: Ultimately, our approach provides a versatile framework for privacy-preserving model adaptation, enabling diffusion models to generalize effectively to novel styles and domains without risking exposure of sensitive data points.
elements.sent-2411-048: Diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising, song2020score, song2020denoising, rombach2022high leverage an iterative denoising process to generate high-quality images that aligns with a given conditional input from random noise.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In text-to-image generation, this conditional input is based on a textual description (a prompt) that guides the model in shaping the image to reflect the content and style specified by the text.
elements.sent-2411-050: To convert the text prompt into a suitable conditional format, it is first tokenized into discrete tokens, each representing a word or sub-word unit.
elements.sent-2411-051: These tokens are then converted into a sequence of embedding vectors $v_i$ that encapsulate the meaning of each token within the model’s semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-052: Next, these embeddings pass through a transformer text encoder, such as CLIP \cite{radford2021learning , outputting a single text-conditional vector $y$ that serves as the conditioning input.}
elements.sent-2411-053: This text-conditioning vector $y$ is then incorporated at each denoising step, guiding the model in aligning the output image with the specific details outlined in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-054: The image generation process, also known as the reverse diffusion process, comprises of $T$ discrete timesteps and starts with pure Gaussian noise $x_T$.
elements.sent-2411-055: At each decreasing timestep $t$, the denoising model, which often utilizes a U-Net structure with cross-attention layers, takes a noisy image $x_t$ and text conditioning $y$ as inputs and predicts the noise component $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$, where $\theta$ denotes the denoising model's parameters.
elements.sent-2411-056: The predicted noise is then used to make a reverse diffusion step from $x_t$ to $x_{t-1}$, iteratively refining the noisy image closer to a coherent output $x_0$ that aligns with the text conditional $y$.
elements.sent-2411-057: In particular, denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIM) sampling \cite{song2020denoising uses the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$ and a noise schedule represented by an array of scalars $\{\alpha_t\}_{t=1 ^T$ to first predict a clean image $\hat{x _0$, then makes a small step in the direction of $\hat{x _0$ to obtain $x_{t-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The reverse diffusion process for DDIM sampling can be formalized as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{x}_0 = \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \label{eq:ddim_predict}\end{equation} \begin{equation}x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\hat{x}_0 + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}}\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t) \label{eq:ddim}\end{equation} The training of a text-conditioned diffusion model involves minimizing a loss function that guides the model to predict the noise added to an image, given both the noisy image $x_t$ and the text conditioning $y$.
elements.sent-2411-059: The objective function is typically a mean squared error (MSE) between the true noise $\epsilon$ and the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$.
elements.sent-2411-060: The denoising model is therefore trained over the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x,\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),t\sim[T]}\lb\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)\|^2\rb \label{eq:ldm_loss}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: Bansal et al. \cite{bansal2023universal propose a universal guidance algorithm that allows the reverse diffusion process to be guided by arbitrary guidance functions, including object detection, image segmentation and facial recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In this section, we formally describe CLIP style guidance, one of the many processes explored in the universal guidance study.
elements.sent-2411-063: We also only describe the forward guidance process.
elements.sent-2411-064: For a generalized and complete description of universal guidance process, including the backward guidance process and per-step self-recurrence, we defer the reader to the original paper.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $x_c$ denote a target image whose style we wish to replicate and $\clip(\cdot)$ denote a CLIP image encoder.
elements.sent-2411-066: To incorporate style guidance, given a noisy image $x_t$, we first compute $\hat{x _0$ using the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$ and Equation \eqref{eq:ddim_predict .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Using $\hat{x _0$, we then compute the following: \begin{equation}\hat{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, y, t) = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t) + w\sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\nabla_{x_t}\ell_{\cos}(\clip(x_c),\clip(\hat{x}_0)) \label{eq:SG_noise}\end{equation} where $w$ is a guidance weight parameter and $\ell_{\cos (\cdot,\cdot)$ is the negative cosine loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We then perform the reverse diffusion step using the same procedure given in Equations \eqref{eq:ddim_predict and \eqref{eq:ddim , but replacing $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$ with $\hat{\epsilon _\theta(x_t, y, t)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Using this reverse diffusion procedure, we generate a high-quality image $x_0$ that aligns with the text conditioning $y$ while also optimizing the cosine similarity between the CLIP embeddings of $x_c$ and $x_0$, resulting in $x_0$ having the stylistic characteristics of $x_c$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Textual Inversion (TI) \cite{gal2023an is an adaptation technique that enables personalization using a small dataset of typically only 3-5 images.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This approach essentially learns a new token that encapsulates the semantic meaning of the training images, allowing the model to associate specific visual features with a custom token.
elements.sent-2411-072: To achieve this, TI trains a new token embedding, denoted as $u$, which represents a placeholder token, often denoted as $S$.
elements.sent-2411-073: During training, images are conditioned of phrases such as ``A photo of $S$'' or ``A painting in the style of $S$''.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, unlike the fixed embeddings of typical tokens $v_i$, $u$ is a learnable parameter.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $y_u$ denote the text conditioning vector resulting from a prompt containing the token $S$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Through gradient descent, TI minimizes the diffusion model loss with respect to $u$, while keeping the diffusion model parameters $\theta$ fixed, iteratively refining this embedding to capture the unique characteristics of the training images.
elements.sent-2411-077: The resulting optimal embedding $u_*$ is formalized as follows: \begin{equation}u_* = \arg\min_u \mathbb{E}_{x,\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),t\sim[T]}[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y_u, t)\|^2]. \label{eq:ti_loss}\end{equation} Hence, $u_*$ represents an optimized placeholder token $S_*$, which can employed in prompts such as ``A photo of $S_*$ floating in space'' or ``A drawing of a capybara in the style of $S_*$'', enabling the generation of personalized images that reflect the learned visual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-078: In this work, we adopt differential privacy (DP) \cite{dwork2006calibrating, dwork2006differential as our privacy framework.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Over the past decade, DP has become the gold standard for privacy protection in both research and industry.
elements.sent-2411-080: It measures the stability of a randomized algorithm concerning changes in an input instance, thereby quantifying the extent to which an adversary can infer the existence of a specific input based on the algorithm's output.
elements.sent-2411-081: When $\delta = 0$, we say $\mcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-pure DP or ($\varepsilon$-DP).
elements.sent-2411-082: To achieve DP, the Gaussian mechanism is often applied \cite{dwork2014algorithmic, balle2018improving , adding Gaussian noise scaled by the sensitivity of the function $f$ and privacy parameters $\varepsilon$ and $\delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Specifically, noise with standard deviation $\sigma = \frac{\Delta_f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta) {\varepsilon}$ is added to the output\footnote{In practice, we use numerical privacy accountant such as \cite{balle2018improving, mironov2017renyi to calibrate the noise.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: When the context is clear, we may omit the subscript $f$.
elements.sent-2411-085: This mechanism enables a smooth privacy-utility tradeoff and is widely used in privacy-preserving machine learning, including in DP-SGD~\cite{abadi2016deep , which applies Gaussian noise during model updates to achieve DP.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Subsampling is a standard technique in DP, where a full dataset of size \( n \) is first subsampled to \( m \) records without replacement (typically with \( m \ll n \)), and the privatization mechanism (such as the Gaussian mechanism) is then applied.
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, if a mechanism provides \((\varepsilon, \delta)\)-DP on a dataset of size \( m \), it achieves \((\varepsilon', \delta')\)-DP on the subsampled dataset, where \(\delta' = \frac{m {n \delta\) and \begin{equation}\label{eq:pabs} \varepsilon' = \log \left( 1 + \frac{m}{n} \left( e^\varepsilon - 1 \right) \right) = O \left( \frac{m}{n} \varepsilon \right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: This result is well-known (see, for example, \cite[Theorem~29]{steinke2022composition ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: Notably, if the DP mechanism is Gaussian, even tighter amplification bounds can be applied \cite{mironov2017renyi .}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Differentially Private Adaptation of Diffusion Models
elements.sent-2411-090: Recent advancements in applying differential privacy (DP) to diffusion models have aimed to balance privacy preservation with the high utility of generative outputs.
elements.sent-2411-091: Dockhorn et al.~\cite{dockhorn2023differentially proposed a differentially private diffusion model (DP diffusion) that enables privacy-preserving generation of realistic samples, setting a foundational approach for adapting diffusion processes under DP constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Another common strategy involves training a model on a large public dataset, followed by differentially private finetuning on a private dataset, as explored by Ghalebikesabi et al.~\cite{ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-093: While effective in certain contexts, this approach raises privacy concerns, particularly around risks of information leakage during the finetuning phase~\cite{pmlr-v235-tramer24a .}
elements.sent-2411-094: In response to these limitations, various adaptation techniques have emerged.
elements.sent-2411-095: Although not specific to diffusion models, some methods focus on training models on synthetic data followed by DP-constrained finetuning, as in the VIP approach~\cite{yu2024vip , which demonstrates the feasibility of applying DP in later adaptation stages.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Other approaches explore differentially private learning of feature representations~\cite{sander2024differentially , aiming to distill private information into a generalized embedding space while maintaining DP guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Although these adaptations are not yet implemented for diffusion models, they lay essential groundwork for developing secure and efficient privacy-preserving generative models.
elements.section-2411-003: Differentially Private Adaptation via Noisy Aggregated Embeddings
elements.sent-2411-098: Let $x^{(1) , \dots, x^{(n)}$ represent a target dataset of images whose characteristics we wish to privately adapt our image generation towards.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Assuming that we can encode each image $x^{(i)}$ as a directional embedding vector $u^{(i)}$, we can aggregate the embeddings $u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}$ by calculating the centroid.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The purpose of this aggregation is to limit the sensitivity of the final output to each $x^{(i)}$.
elements.sent-2411-101: In order to provide DP guanrantees, we also add isotropic Gaussian noise to the centroid.
elements.sent-2411-102: We can therefore define the resulting embedding vector $u^*$ as follows: \begin{equation}u^* = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}u^{(i)} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2I) \label{eq:centroid_method}\end{equation} where the minimum $\sigma$ required to provide $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP is given by the following expression based on \cite[Theorem~1]{balle2018improving : \begin{equation}\sigma = \frac{\Delta}{n}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2\log(1.25)/\delta}}{\varepsilon}. \label{eq:sigma}\end{equation} In the context of our problem, $\Delta = \sup_{i,j}\|u^{(i) - u^{(j)}\|$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Since our embedding vectors are directional, we can normalize each $u^{(i)}$, allowing us to set $\Delta = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The noisy centroid embedding $u^*$ can then be used to adapt the downstream image generation process.
elements.sent-2411-105: In Sections 3.3 and 3.4, we describe how to apply our method to TI and SG respectively, outlining the encoding of $x^{(i)}$ as $u^{(i)}$ and the role of $u^*$ in the image generation process.
elements.sent-2411-106: To reduce the amount of noise needed to provide the same level of DP, we employ subsampling: instead of computing the centroid over all $n$ embedding vectors, we randomly sample $m \le n$ embedding vectors without replacement and compute the centroid over only the sampled vectors.
elements.sent-2411-107: Then the standard privacy amplification by subsampling bounds (such as \eqref{eq:pabs ) can be applied.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Formally, we sample $\Dsub \subseteq \{u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}\}$ where $|\Dsub| = m$, and compute the output embedding as follows: \begin{equation}u^* = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{u^{(i)} \in \Dsub}u^{(i)} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2I), \label{eq:centroid_subsample}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ can be computed numerically for any target $\varepsilon, \delta$ and subsampling rate $\frac{m {n}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Applying our method to style guidance is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-110: To obtain the embeddings, we simply use the CLIP image encoder to obtain the embedding for each image.
elements.sent-2411-112: We can then aggregate the embeddings $u^{(i)}$ to obtain $u^*$ via Equation \eqref{eq:centroid_method or \eqref{eq:centroid_subsample , depending on whether we wish to incorporate subsampling.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Finally, we use $u^*$ to guide the reverse diffusion process, as outlined in Section 2.1.1, by computing $\hat{\epsilon _\theta(x_t, y, t)$ via a modification of Equation \eqref{eq:ddim : \begin{equation}\hat{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, y, t) = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t) + w\sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\nabla_{x_t}\ell_{\cos}(u^*, \clip(\hat{x}_0)) \label{eq:DPSG}\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-123: The first dataset consists of 158 artworks created by the artist {\eve , who has provided consent for non-commercial use.}
elements.sent-2411-124: This dataset serves to examine how models can capture and replicate artistic styles without directly copying individual works.
elements.sent-2411-125: Although we acknowledge the possibility that a few of these artworks may have been inadvertently included in the pre-training of Stable Diffusion due their public presence on social media platforms, the artist’s limited recognition and modest portfolio size make it unlikely that their unique style could be effectively extracted from the pre-trained model.

elements.sent-2411-127: These pictograms were unveiled in February 2023, several months after the release of Stable Diffusion v1.5, which serves as the base model for our experiments.

elements.sent-2411-139: Our primary goal is to investigate how DP configurations, specifically the $\varepsilon$ and subsampling sizes ($m$), affect the generated images' quality and privacy resilience.
elements.sent-2411-140: For regular TI, we utilize Stable Diffusion's default process to embed the private dataset without any additional noise.
elements.sent-2411-141: For the DP-enhanced experiments using our method, we test multiple configurations of $m$ and $\varepsilon$ to analyze the trade-off between image fidelity and privacy resilience.

elements.sent-2411-158: We presented a differentially private adaptation approach for diffusion models using embedding-based methods--Universal Guidance and Textual Inversion (TI)--to enable privacy-preserving style transfer from small, sensitive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-159: Through experiments on a private artwork dataset and the Paris 2024 Olympics pictograms, we demonstrated that our approach maintains high stylistic fidelity while providing strong privacy guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-160: Notably, TI proved more effective in capturing detailed stylistic elements compared to SG, especially with added noise and subsampling to protect privacy.
elements.sent-2411-161: Our findings suggest that embedding-driven methods can serve as efficient and scalable alternatives to DP-SGD for privacy-preserving model adaptation, minimizing computational demands and offering resilience against privacy attacks.
elements.sent-2411-162: This approach provides a balance between style quality and privacy, enabling generative models to adapt responsibly to new domains.

elements.sent-2411-164: We introduce a novel method for adapting diffusion models under differential privacy (DP) constraints, enabling privacy-preserving style and content transfer without fine-tuning model weights.
elements.sent-2411-165: Traditional approaches to private adaptation, such as DP-SGD, incur significant computational and memory overhead when applied to large, complex models.
elements.sent-2411-166: In addition, when adapting to small-scale specialized datasets, DP-SGD incurs large amount of noise that significantly degrades the performance.
elements.sent-2411-167: Our approach instead leverages an embedding-based technique derived from Textual Inversion (TI) and adapted with differentially private mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-168: We apply TI to Stable Diffusion for style adaptation using two private datasets: a collection of artworks by a single artist and pictograms from the Paris 2024 Olympics.
elements.sent-2411-169: Experimental results show that the TI-based adaptation achieves superior fidelity in style transfer, even under strong privacy guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-170: Notably, tools such as Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high, podell2023sdxl have been made readily accessible for general use.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Building on this foundation, efficient adaptation methods such as parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods~\cite{hu2022lora,von-platen-etal-2022-diffusers,ruiz2023dreambooth , guidance based approaches~\cite{ho2021classifierfree, kim2022diffusionclip, bansal2023universal , and pseudo-word generation~\cite{gal2023an enable users to leverage this extensive pretraining for customizing models that can specialize on downstream tasks with smaller datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: The rapid adoption of diffusion models has raised significant privacy and legal concerns.
elements.sent-2411-173: These models are vulnerable to privacy attacks, such as membership inference~\cite{duan2023diffusion , where attackers determine if a specific data point was used for training, and data extraction~\cite{carlini2023extracting , which enables reconstruction of training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: This risk is amplified during fine-tuning on smaller, domain-specific datasets, where each record has a greater impact.
elements.sent-2411-175: Additionally, reliance on large datasets scraped without consent raises copyright concerns~\cite{vyas2023provable , as diffusion models can reproduce original artworks without credit or compensation.}
elements.sent-2411-176: These issues highlight the urgent need for privacy-preserving technologies and clearer ethical and legal guidelines for generative models.
elements.sent-2411-177: One standard approach for ensuring DP in deep learning is Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD)~\cite{abadi2016deep , which modifies traditional SGD by adding noise to clipped gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-178: However, applying DP-SGD to train diffusion models poses several challenges.
elements.sent-2411-179: It introduces significant computational and memory overhead due to per-sample gradient clipping \cite{hoory2021learning , which is essential for bounding gradient sensitivity~\cite{dwork2006calibrating, abadi2016deep .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: DP-SGD is also incompatible with batch-wise operations like batch normalization, as these link samples and hinder sensitivity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-181: Furthermore, training large models with DP-SGD often leads to substantial performance degradation, particularly under realistic privacy budgets since the required noise scales with the gradient norm.
elements.sent-2411-182: Consequently, existing diffusion models trained with DP-SGD are limited to relatively small-scale images.~\cite{dockhorn2023differentially, ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-183: As a result, recent research has focused on privacy-preserving strategies for adapting diffusion models without the need for full DP-SGD training.
elements.sent-2411-184: One approach adapts large, publicly pre-trained models to new domains under DP constraints, leveraging their representational strengths while reducing computational and memory costs~\cite{ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Similarly, PEFT methods like DP-LoRA~\cite{yu2021differentially fine-tune a small subset of parameters, enabling efficient adaptation with lower privacy costs.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Methods like DP-RDM~\cite{lebensold2024dp avoid direct model updates by using retrieval mechanisms that condition image generation on private data retrieved during inference.}
elements.sent-2411-187: However, these alternate approaches often fail to capture the detail of style, underscoring the challenges of balancing privacy, efficiency, and generative performance.
elements.sent-2411-188: Independent of privacy concerns, Textual Inversion (TI)~\cite{gal2023an provides an effective method for adapting diffusion models to specific styles or content without modifying the model.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Instead, TI learns an external embedding vector that captures the style or content of a target image set, which is then incorporated into text prompts to guide the model’s outputs.
elements.sent-2411-190: A key advantage of TI is its ability to compress a style into a compact vector, reducing computational and memory demands while simplifying the application of privacy-preserving mechanisms, as privacy constraints can be applied directly to embeddings rather than the full model.
elements.sent-2411-191: Additionally, since TI avoids direct model optimization, it remains efficient and compatible with DP constraints on smaller datasets.
elements.sent-2411-192: In this work, we propose a novel privacy-preserving adaptation method for smaller datasets, leveraging TI to avoid the extensive model updates required by DP-SGD or DP-PEFT approaches.
elements.sent-2411-193: Standard TI inherently compresses the target dataset into a single, low-dimensional vector, providing some obfuscation benefits, but it does not offer formal privacy guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-194: To address this limitation, we introduce a private variant of TI, called Differentially Private Aggregation via Textual Inversion (DPAgg-TI) and summarize it in \cref{fig:main .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Our method decouples interactions among samples by learning a separate embedding for each target image, which are then aggregated into a noisy centroid.
elements.sent-2411-196: This approach ensures efficient and secure adaptation to private datasets.
elements.sent-2411-197: Our experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of DPAgg-TI, showing that TI remains robust in preserving stylistic fidelity even under privacy constraints.
elements.sent-2411-198: Applying our method to a private artwork collection by {\eve and Paris 2024 Olympics pictograms~\cite{Paris2024Pictograms , we show that DPAgg-TI captures nuanced stylistic elements while ensuring privacy.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: We observe a trade-off between privacy (controlled by DP parameter $\varepsilon$) and image quality: lower $\varepsilon$ reduces fidelity but maintains the target style under moderate noise.
elements.sent-2411-200: Subsampling further amplifies privacy by reducing sensitivity to individual data points, mitigating noise impact on image quality.
elements.sent-2411-201: This framework enables privacy-preserving adaptation of diffusion models to new styles and domains while protecting sensitive data.
elements.sent-2411-202: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-203: We propose DPAgg-TI that ensures privacy by learning separate embeddings for individual images and aggregating them into a noisy centroid.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our approach enables style adaptation without extensive model updates, reducing computational overhead while preserving privacy.
elements.sent-2411-205: We analyze the trade-off between privacy and image quality, showing that moderate noise maintains stylistic fidelity while protecting sensitive data.
elements.sent-2411-206: We validate our method on diverse datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing stylistic elements under privacy constraints.
elements.sent-2411-207: Diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising, song2020score, song2020denoising, rombach2022high leverage an iterative denoising process to generate high-quality images that align with a given conditional input from random noise.}
elements.sent-2411-208: To convert the text prompt into a suitable conditional format, it is first broken down into discrete tokens, each representing a word or sub-word unit.
elements.sent-2411-209: This vector $y$ is then incorporated at each denoising step, guiding the model to align the output image with the specific details outlined in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-210: The objective function for a text-conditioned diffusion model, given both the noisy image $x_t$ and the text conditioning $y$, is typically a mean squared error (MSE) between the true noise $\epsilon$ and the predicted noise $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)$.
elements.sent-2411-211: The denoising model is therefore trained over the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x,\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),t\sim[T]}[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y, t)\|^2]. \label{eq:ldm_loss}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-212: Textual Inversion (TI) \cite{gal2023an is an adaptation technique that enables personalization using a small dataset of typically 3-5 images.}
elements.sent-2411-213: To achieve this, TI trains a new token embedding, denoted as $u$, representing a placeholder token, denoted as $S$.
elements.sent-2411-214: During training, images are conditioned on phrases such as ``A photo of $S$'' or ``A painting in the style of $S$''.
elements.sent-2411-215: Through gradient descent, TI minimizes the diffusion model loss given in \eqref{eq:ldm_loss with respect to $u$, while keeping the diffusion model parameters $\theta$ fixed, iteratively refining this embedding to capture the unique characteristics of the training images.}
elements.sent-2411-216: It measures the stability of a randomized algorithm with respect to changes in an input instance, thereby quantifying the extent to which an adversary can infer the existence of a specific input based on the algorithm's output.
elements.sent-2411-217: Subsampling is a standard technique in DP, where a full dataset of size \( n \) is first subsampled to \( m \) records without replacement (typically with \( m \ll n \)) before the privatization mechanism (such as the Gaussian mechanism) is applied.
elements.sent-2411-218: This result is well-known (\citet[Theorem~29]{steinke2022composition ), with tighter amplification bounds available for Gaussian mechanisms~\cite{mironov2017renyi .}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Recent advancements in applying DP to diffusion models have aimed to balance privacy preservation with the high utility of generative outputs.
elements.sent-2411-220: Dockhorn et al.~\cite{dockhorn2023differentially proposed a Differentially Private Diffusion Model (DPDM) that enables privacy-preserving generation of realistic samples, setting a foundational approach for adapting diffusion processes using DP-SGD.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Another common strategy involves training a model on a large public dataset, followed by differentially private fine-tuning on a private dataset, as explored by \citet{ghalebikesabi2023differentially .}
elements.sent-2411-222: While effective in certain contexts, this approach raises privacy concerns, particularly around risks of information leakage during the fine-tuning phase~\cite{pmlr-v235-tramer24a .}
elements.sent-2411-223: Although not specific to diffusion models, some methods focus on training models on synthetic data followed by DP-constrained fine-tuning, as in the VIP approach~\cite{yu2024vip , which demonstrates the feasibility of applying DP in later adaptation stages.}
elements.section-2411-007: Differentially Private Adaptation via Textual Inversion
elements.sent-2411-224: TI is inherently parameter-efficient and offers certain privacy benefits, as information from an entire dataset of images is compressed into a single token embedding vector.
elements.sent-2411-225: A similar adaptation technique with privacy guarantees may therefore be desirable.
elements.sent-2411-226: Instead of training a single token embedding on the entire dataset as in regular TI, we train a separate embedding $u^{(i)}$ on each $x^{(i)}$ to obtain a set of embeddings $u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}$, as illustrated in \cref{fig:main .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: We can formalize the encoding process as follows: \begin{equation}u^{(i)} = \arg\min_u \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),t}[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_t^{(i)}, y_u, t)\|^2]. \label{eq:indiv_loss}\end{equation} Then, we can aggregate the embeddings $u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}$ by calculating the centroid.}
elements.sent-2411-228: We can therefore define the resulting embedding vector $u^*$ as follows: \begin{equation}u^* = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}u^{(i)} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2I), \label{eq:centroid_method}\end{equation} where the minimum $\sigma$ required to provide $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP is given by the following expression based on \citet[Theorem~1]{balle2018improving : \begin{equation}\sigma = \frac{\Delta}{n}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-229: In the context of our problem, $\Delta = \sup_{i,j}\|u^{(i) - u^{(j)}\|$.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Similar to regular TI's $u_*$, we can use $u^*$ to represent a new placeholder token $S^*$ that can be incorporated into prompts for personalized image generation.
elements.sent-2411-231: While $u^*$ may not fully solve the TI optimization problem presented in \eqref{eq:ti_loss , it provides provable privacy guarantees, with only a minimal trade-off in accurately representing the style of the target dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-232: We compiled two datasets to evaluate our style adaptation method, specifically selecting content unlikely to be recognized by Stable Diffusion v1.5, our base model.
elements.sent-2411-233: The first dataset consists of 158 artworks by the artist {\eve , who has granted consent for non-commercial use.}
elements.sent-2411-234: This dataset allows us to assess whether models can capture artistic styles without memorizing individual works.
elements.sent-2411-235: While some of these artworks may have been publicly accessible on social media, making incidental inclusion in Stable Diffusion’s pretraining possible, the artist’s limited recognition and relatively small portfolio reduce the likelihood that the model has internalized her unique style.
elements.sent-2411-236: This dataset serves as a controlled test for privacy-preserving style transfer on individual artistic collections.
elements.sent-2411-237: The second dataset contains 47 pictograms from the Paris 2024 Olympics~\cite{Paris2024Pictograms , permitted strictly for non-commercial editorial use~\cite{IOCProperties .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: These pictograms were officially released in February 2023, several months after the release of Stable Diffusion v1.5, ensuring they were absent from the model’s pretraining data.
elements.sent-2411-239: This dataset allows us to assess how well our approach adapts to newly introduced visual styles that the base model has never encountered.
elements.sent-2411-240: Both datasets are used to test the ability of our method to extract and transfer stylistic elements while preserving privacy.

elements.sent-2411-275: We extend our approach to style guidance (SG) by leveraging the framework of Universal Guidance~\cite{bansal2023universal .}
elements.sent-2411-276: Specifically, we focus on CLIP-based style guidance, which optimizes the similarity between the CLIP embeddings of a target image and the generated image.
elements.sent-2411-277: We encode each target image \(x^{(i)}\) as \(u^{(i)}\) via a CLIP image encoder, then aggregate the embeddings \(u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}\) into \(u^*\) using \eqref{eq:centroid_method or \eqref{eq:centroid_subsample , depending on whether subsampling is applied.}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: The aggregated embedding \(u^*\) is then incorporated into the reverse diffusion process as a style guide.
elements.sent-2411-279: Further implementation detailes are provided in Appendix~\ref{app:Style Guidance .}
elements.sent-2411-280: We apply our SG-based approach to both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-281: While it provides privacy protection by obfuscating embedding details, the resulting images captured only generalized stylistic elements and lack the detailed fidelity and coherence achieved with the TI-based method.
elements.sent-2411-282: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:sg-results , this highlights the superiority of TI in balancing privacy and high-quality image generation.}
elements.sent-2411-283: The reduced effectiveness of SG for style transfer may stem from its sensitivity to hyperparameters such as the guidance weight $w$, leading to instability.
elements.sent-2411-284: Although \citet{bansal2023universal proposed remedies, namely backward guidance and per-step self-recurrence, these proved insufficient for our application.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Additionally, the CLIP embeddings may not retain enough stylistic detail after the aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-286: We presented a differentially private adaptation method for diffusion models using Textual Inversion for privacy-preserving style transfer.

elements.sent-2411-345: Personalizing large-scale diffusion models poses serious privacy risks, especially when adapting to small, sensitive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-346: A common approach is to fine-tune the model using differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD), but this suffers from severe utility degradation due to the high noise needed for privacy, particularly in the small data regime.
elements.sent-2411-347: We propose an alternative that leverages Textual Inversion (TI), which learns an embedding vector for an image or set of images, to enable adaptation under differential privacy (DP) constraints.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our approach, Differentially Private Aggregation via Textual Inversion (DPAgg-TI), adds calibrated noise to the aggregation of per-image embeddings to ensure formal DP guarantees while preserving high output fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-349: We show that DPAgg-TI outperforms DP-SGD finetuning in both utility and robustness under the same privacy budget, achieving results closely matching the non-private baseline on style adaptation tasks using private artwork from a single artist and Paris 2024 Olympic pictograms.
elements.sent-2411-350: In contrast, DP-SGD fails to generate meaningful outputs in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-351: The rapid adoption of diffusion models ~\cite{ho2020denoising, song2020score, rombach2022high has raised significant privacy and legal concerns.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Differential privacy~(DP)~\cite{dwork2006differential is a widely adopted framework for addressing these challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-353: In this work, we propose a novel privacy-preserving adaptation method for smaller datasets, leveraging TI to avoid the extensive model updates required by DP-SGD.
elements.sent-2411-354: Standard TI does not offer formal privacy guarantees, so to address this limitation, we introduce a private variant of TI, called Differentially Private Aggregation via Textual Inversion (DPAgg-TI) and summarize it in Figure \ref{fig:main .}
elements.sent-2411-355: The resulting optimal embedding $u^*$ is formalized as follows: \begin{equation}u^* = \arg\min_u \mathbb{E}_{x,\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),t\sim[T]}[\|\epsilon-\epsilon_\theta(x_t, y_u, t)\|^2]. \label{eq:ti_loss}\end{equation} Hence, $u^*$ represents an optimized placeholder token $S^*$, which can employed in prompts such as ``A photo of $S^*$ floating in space'' or ``A drawing of a capybara in the style of $S^*$'', enabling the generation of personalized images that reflect the learned visual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-356: Dockhorn et al.~\citet{dockhorn2023differentially proposed a Differentially Private Diffusion Model (DPDM) that enables privacy-preserving generation of realistic samples, setting a foundational approach for adapting diffusion processes using DP-SGD.}

Right: Paris 2024 Olympic pictograms ($n = 47$), \textcopyright~International Olympic Committee, 2023.} \label{fig:datasets}
elements.sent-2411-357: Instead of training a single token embedding on the entire dataset as in regular TI, we train a separate embedding $u^{(i)}$ on each $x^{(i)}$ to obtain a set of embeddings $u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}$, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:main .}}
elements.sent-2411-358: In order to provide DP guarantees, we also add isotropic Gaussian noise to the centroid.
elements.sent-2411-359: We can therefore define the resulting embedding vector $u^*_{\text{DP }$ as follows: \begin{equation}u^*_{\text{DP}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}u^{(i)} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2I), \label{eq:centroid_method}\end{equation} where the minimum $\sigma$ required to provide $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP is given by the following expression based on \citet[Theorem~1]{balle2018improving : \begin{equation}\sigma = \frac{\Delta}{n}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-360: The noisy centroid embedding $u^*_{\text{DP }$ can then be used to adapt the downstream image generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-361: Similar to regular TI's $u^*$, we can use $u^*_{\text{DP }$ to represent a new placeholder token $S^*$ that can be incorporated into prompts for personalized image generation.}
elements.sent-2411-362: While $u^*_{\text{DP }$ may not fully solve the TI optimization problem presented in \eqref{eq:ti_loss , it provides provable privacy guarantees, with only a minimal trade-off in accurately representing the style of the target dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-363: Formally, we sample $\Dsub \subseteq \{u^{(1) , \dots, u^{(n)}\}$ where $|\Dsub| = m$, and compute the output embedding as follows: \begin{equation}u^*_{\text{DP}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{u^{(i)} \in \Dsub}u^{(i)} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2I), \label{eq:centroid_subsample}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ can be computed numerically for any target $\varepsilon, \delta$ and subsampling rate $\frac{m {n}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-364: We used the same random seed to generate embeddings, subsample images, and sample DP noise for ease of visual comparison between different configurations.

elements.figure-2411-030: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{images/ablation2.2.png} \includegraphics[width=0.99\columnwidth]{images/ablation3.2.png} \caption{Comparing our approach to applying DP-SGD to regular TI using prompts ``an icon of a dragon in the style of the Paris 2024 Olympic Pictograms'' and ``a painting of Taylor Swift in the style of {\eve}'' respectively. elements.sent-2411-376: Our proposed mechanism can also be interpreted through the lens of copyright protection.
elements.sent-2411-377: This connection is grounded in the framework of Near Access-Freeness (NAF) \citep{vyas2023provable , which evaluates whether a model’s outputs reveal undue influence from specific data points by comparing them to those from a safe model trained without access to the same data.}
elements.sent-2411-378: Since DPAgg-TI satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP, it also satisfies $\varepsilon$-NAF, which means the adapted model behaves similarly to one that never saw the private images, under the NAF criterion.
elements.sent-2411-379: However, we emphasize that this guarantee holds only within the NAF framework; it does not constitute a general claim about content similarity or legal compliance.
elements.sent-2411-380: Crucially, DPAgg-TI is designed to adapt to the style of private images, not their specific content.
elements.sent-2411-381: Prior work and legal precedent suggest that style imitation is generally considered fair use and does not constitute infringement \citep{vyas2023provable .}
elements.sent-2411-382: Thus, our mechanism aligns with the intended protections of NAF: it avoids memorization while still enabling meaningful personalization and stylistic adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-383: We defer the details of copyright protection to Appendix~\ref{app:copyright .}
elements.sent-2411-384: We presented a differentially private adaptation method for diffusion models based on Textual Inversion, enabling privacy-preserving style transfer without the need for full model fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-385: By learning per-image embeddings and aggregating them with calibrated noise, our method, DPAgg-TI, achieves strong formal privacy guarantees while maintaining high output fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-386: Experiments on private artwork and Paris 2024 pictograms show that DPAgg-TI consistently outperforms DP-SGD, which fails to produce meaningful results under comparable privacy budgets.
elements.sent-2411-387: These results highlight the effectiveness of embedding-level adaptation as an efficient and scalable alternative to traditional gradient-based approaches, especially in low-data regimes.
elements.sent-2411-388: Unlike DP-SGD, which introduces significant computational overhead and utility degradation, DPAgg-TI is lightweight, modular, and compatible with existing diffusion backbones.
elements.sent-2411-389: Our findings suggest that embedding-centric approaches offer a promising direction for privacy-aware personalization, and motivate further research into cross-modal extensions, improved aggregation techniques, and integration with broader privacy-preserving frameworks.

elements.sent-2411-399: Modern generative models typically produce outputs via randomized sampling.
elements.sent-2411-400: Leveraging this inherent randomness, \citet{vyas2023provable introduced Near Access-Freeness (NAF) as a metric to quantify the similarity between a model’s output and copyrighted content.}
elements.sent-2411-401: The key idea is to compare the output distribution of a potentially infringing model to that of a safe model -- one trained without access to the target content.
elements.sent-2411-402: Formally, let $\msf{safe}$ be a mapping from a data point $x \in \mathcal{C}$ (where $\mcal{C}$ is the collection of copyrighted samples) to a generative model $\msf{safe (x) \in \mathcal{W}$ that is trained without using $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-403: A canonical example is the leave-one-out-safe model, trained on the full dataset excluding $x$.
elements.sent-2411-404: Since $\msf{safe (x)$ does not have access to $x$, the probability that it generates content resembling $x$ is exponentially small.}
elements.sent-2411-405: Any such resemblance is considered fortuitous.
elements.sent-2411-406: Formally, the NAF criterion is defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-407: Given a mapping $\msf{safe : \mathcal{C \rightarrow \mathcal{W}$ and a divergence measure $\Delta$, we say a model $w$ is $k_y$-near access-free (or $k_y$-NAF) on prompt $ y \in \msf{Y}$ if for every $x \in \mathcal{C}$, \begin{equation}\Delta\left( p(\cdot|y) \, \| \, \msf{safe}_x(\cdot|y) \right) \leq k_y.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: If $k_y = 0$, the model is indistinguishable from a safe model, meaning any resemblance to copyrighted material is by random chance.
elements.sent-2411-409: More generally, a small $k_y$ suggests the model is unlikely to generate outputs resembling $x$ with higher probability than a model that has never seen $x$.
elements.sent-2411-410: NAF is closely related to concepts in Differential Privacy (DP) \citep{elkin2023can .}
elements.sent-2411-411: Depending on the divergence measure $\Delta$, NAF resembles different DP variants -- for example, $\varepsilon$-DP when $\Delta = \Delta_{\mathrm{max }$ \citep{dwork2006calibrating , and $(1,\varepsilon)$-R\'enyi DP when $\Delta = \Delta_{\mathrm{KL }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-412: Translating DP to generative models yields the following definition:.
elements.sent-2411-413: Denote by $P_S(\cdot|y)$ the distribution over outputs generated by a model trained on, or adapted from, $S$ with algorithm $\mathcal{A}$, where randomness includes both training and generation stages.
elements.sent-2411-414: The generation is said to satisfy $\varepsilon$-Differentially Private Generation ($\varepsilon$-DPG) if for every $y \in \mathcal{Y}$, \begin{equation}\Delta\left( P_S(\cdot|y) \, \| \, P_{S'}(\cdot|y) \right) \leq \varepsilon.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-415: Here, neighboring datasets differ by a single data point (or privacy unit).
elements.sent-2411-416: If the training process is $\varepsilon$-DP, then the outputs naturally satisfy $\varepsilon$-DPG via the data processing inequality.
elements.sent-2411-417: One benefit of DPG is the flexibility to add noise during generation rather than training, potentially improving the utility-privacy tradeoff.
elements.sent-2411-419: Also, under DP, the trained model can be released, but under DPG, only the outputs are safe to share.
elements.sent-2411-420: This asymmetry in NAF can enable better utility.
elements.sent-2411-421: Additionally, NAF allows more flexibility in choosing the safe model, which can be exploited in algorithm design.
elements.sent-2411-422: Given these conceptual overlaps, both DP-SGD based training and our proposed private adaptation method DPAgg-TI satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP, so they naturally satisfy $\varepsilon$-NAF with the leave-one-out safe model.
elements.sent-2411-423: We emphasize that this guarantee is meaningful only within the formal framework of NAF.
elements.sent-2411-424: It does not imply broader legal immunity or empirical indistinguishability from the original content.
elements.sent-2411-425: However, within this framework, satisfying $\varepsilon$-NAF allows us to argue that any close resemblance between outputs and private training data is no more likely than would be expected from a model that never had access to that data.
elements.sent-2411-426: This theoretical grounding supports the privacy and safety claims of our adaptation method.
elements.sent-2411-427: Importantly, the goal of DPAgg-TI is to adapt to the style of a private image set—not its precise content.
elements.sent-2411-428: This distinction matters: style transfer is widely considered to fall under the doctrine of fair use, particularly in artistic and creative contexts.
elements.sent-2411-429: As discussed in \citet{elkin2023can and further elaborated in legal analysis such as \citet{carlini2023extracting , generating new content in the style of a work, without reproducing its substantive elements, is generally not considered copyright infringement.}}
elements.sent-2411-430: Therefore, the use of DPAgg-TI to learn and reproduce stylistic attributes does not contradict the spirit or intent of the NAF framework.
elements.sent-2411-431: Instead, it offers a promising direction for responsibly fine-tuning generative models on private or copyrighted sources while respecting both privacy and intellectual property boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-432: Direct comparisons of computational cost across methods are inherently challenging due to differing training paradigms, optimization procedures, and parameter settings.
elements.sent-2411-433: Nonetheless, to provide a concrete sense of scale, we report representative computational costs for each method based on experiments conducted using a Stable Diffusion v1.5 model on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU.
